translation: Woe to those who give less [than due]
commentary:
**وَيْلٌ لِّلْمُطَفِّفِينَ**

*Woe to those who give less [than due].*

The surah opens with one of the most powerful expressions of divine anger in the Quran: **وَيْلٌ**. This word carries tremendous weight in Arabic, originally formed from two words that conveyed the severest forms of curse and condemnation. When Arabs used this expression, they invoked the worst possible misfortune upon someone - whether directed at another in anger or even at themselves in moments of extreme distress.

What makes this opening particularly striking is its grammatical structure. In standard Arabic, we would expect the sentence to begin with a verb: "Wayl is for the mutaffifin." But by placing **وَيْلٌ** at the very beginning, Allah ﷻ creates multiple effects. First, it generates shock and captures immediate attention. Second, it builds mystery - we know something terrible awaits these people before we even learn who they are. Most importantly, this structure transforms the sentence into an exclamation rather than a mere statement of fact. The Quran doesn't use punctuation marks in the way modern texts do; instead, its language itself contains the punctuation. This **وَيْلٌ** is the Quran's exclamation mark, expressing divine rage in its very structure.

The intensity of this opening suggests this surah addresses a community that has received extensive warnings. This is not the soft invitation of early revelation but the stern rebuke that comes after repeated reminders have been ignored. This linguistic fury directed at those who commit seemingly minor business infractions reveals how seriously Allah ﷻ takes even small acts of injustice.

The word **الْمُطَفِّفِينَ** comes from the root طَفَّفَ, which refers to taking or giving just a little extra - the small amount that spills over the top when filling a container, or conversely, the slight reduction when scraping across the surface. This root describes the merchant who, when measuring goods for a customer, uses a slightly curved blade to scrape the top, taking a bit from inside the container as well. The customer receives 99% or 99.5% instead of 100% - a difference so small they don't notice the theft.

But **تطفيف** works both ways. It also describes the aggressive consumer who demands every last bit of what they paid for, who haggers relentlessly, who bullies sellers into giving more than agreed, who will stand in return lines for hours to claim every penny of their rights. The mutaffifin are those who, as customers, fight tooth and nail for 100% of what they deserve, but as sellers or service providers, quietly skim off the top, confident no one will notice.

This behavior extends far beyond ancient markets. Today it manifests in contractors who deliberately slow their work to extend billing periods, in companies that exploit labor in countries with weak regulations, in professionals who provide substandard service while charging premium rates, in corporations that bury deceptive clauses in microscopic contract text that requires a law degree to understand. The essence of tatfif is taking advantage of someone's lack of knowledge or desperate circumstances to extract maximum benefit while giving minimum return.

translation: Who, when they take a measure from people, take in full
commentary:
**ٱلَّذِينَ إِذَا ٱكْتَالُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ يَسْتَوْفُونَ**

*Who, when they take a measure from people, take in full.*

Now Allah ﷻ describes the first side of their hypocrisy. The phrase **ٱكْتَالُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ** is significant - the preposition **عَلَى** (upon) suggests dominance and bullying. These aren't simply customers making purchases; they're consumers who use their position to pressure and intimidate sellers. 

Imagine the desperate vendor outside the Haram trying to sell simple wooden toys to feed his children, and the wealthy tourist who makes five times what this man earns in a year yet haggles him down to nearly nothing, walking away with the product while the seller barely covers his costs. Or consider modern corporate buyers who strong-arm manufacturers: "We can get this cheaper overseas where there are no labor laws. Match that price or we walk." The small business owner, barely surviving, has no choice but to accept margins so thin they can hardly sustain themselves.

The word **يَسْتَوْفُونَ** emphasizes their determination to extract every last bit of value. These are people who will spend five hours on hold with customer service, who will stand in return lines until they get satisfaction, who will fight for gift certificates and manager apologies. There's nothing inherently wrong with knowing your rights as a consumer - the problem arises in the stark contrast with how these same people behave when the roles reverse.

translation: But if they give by measure or by weight to them, they cause loss
commentary:
**وَإِذَا كَالُوهُمْ أَو وَّزَنُوهُمْ يُخْسِرُونَ**

*But if they give by measure or by weight to them, they cause loss.*

The language here is remarkably precise. **كَالُوهُمْ** refers to measuring large quantities with scales, while **وَزَنُوهُمْ** refers to weighing smaller amounts, often currency itself (since ancient coins weren't uniformly minted and had to be weighed to determine their actual silver or gold content). Together, these words encompass both the product being sold and the payment being received - every aspect of the transaction.

But notice something profound: the expected Arabic would be **كَالُوا لَهُمْ** - "they measure for them." Instead, Allah ﷻ says **كَالُوهُمْ** - the lam is missing. This grammatical shift suggests these merchants aren't just weighing products; they're weighing the people themselves, sizing them up, evaluating them. 

This brings to mind the Chinese furniture market where vendors keep secret charts: if the customer is a white American, charge this price; if British, charge that; if Pakistani, another price; if they seem knowledgeable, one rate; if they appear ignorant, inflate it. The mechanic who looks at a customer unfamiliar with cars and suddenly discovers a need for a new transmission, but would only suggest an oil change if you clearly know your vehicles - this is **كَالُوهُمْ**, weighing the person rather than just the product.

The word **يُخْسِرُونَ** - "they cause loss" - is deliberately open-ended. Allah ﷻ doesn't specify they cause loss to their customers, though that's obviously true. The phrasing suggests something broader: they cause loss to society as a whole. When cheating becomes normalized, when everyone says "that's just how business works," when newcomers are advised "you have to play the game to survive," an entire culture of corruption takes root. The tatfif doesn't just harm individual transactions; it corrupts the moral fabric of commerce itself.

And there's a spiritual dimension too: **يُخْسِرُونَ** includes the loss they inflict upon their own souls. They think they're winning, accumulating wealth, getting ahead. In reality, they're destroying themselves, earning Allah's wrath with every transaction, building their lives and homes with stolen pennies that will testify against them.

translation: Do they not think that they will be resurrected
commentary:
**أَلَا يَظُنُّ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَنَّهُم مَّبْعُوثُونَ**

*Do they not think that they will be resurrected*

After describing their crime, Allah ﷻ immediately connects it to a fundamental failure of faith. The word **يَظُنُّ** means "to think" or "to consider" - it hasn't even crossed their minds, not for a moment. The thought of being held accountable simply doesn't occur to them.

The word **مَّبْعُوثُونَ** is particularly evocative. **بَعْثَ** means to be raised up, but it also carries the meaning of being summoned, brought forth, presented before authority - like being called to appear in court. If we maintain the imagery the surah has been building, we've been watching someone commit financial crimes, and now Allah ﷻ asks: does it not occur to this criminal that he will be brought to trial?

This reveals something crucial: the person who commits tatfif doesn't consider it a serious crime. They've stolen so little, so subtly, that surely there's no need for concern. No one bled, no one called the police, there's no investigation. It's just business. But Allah ﷻ is revealing that this "minor" offense provokes His severe anger precisely because the perpetrator doesn't take it seriously, doesn't imagine themselves accountable, doesn't think they'll ever be caught and brought to justice.

translation: For a tremendous Day
commentary:
**لِيَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ**

*For a tremendous Day*

The **لِ** here indicates purpose or appointment - they will be brought forth for a specific day, at a designated time. Continuing the courtroom imagery, this is the trial date, the appointed hour when they must appear. The criminal who thinks he'll never get caught suddenly has a court date set.

But this isn't any ordinary day of judgment. It's **يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ** - a tremendous, magnificent, overwhelming day. The word **عَظِيمٍ** conveys grandeur, magnitude, something so vast it inspires awe and terror. This is the day when the true weight of every action, every penny stolen, every margin skimmed, every contract violated will be laid bare.

translation: The Day when mankind will stand before the Lord of the worlds
commentary:
**يَوْمَ يَقُومُ ٱلنَّاسُ لِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ**

*The Day when all people will stand before the Lord of the worlds.*

The scene now unfolds with cinematic precision. **يَقُومُ ٱلنَّاسُ** - all people will stand. This is the courtroom scene fully realized: everyone on their feet, the judge presiding. In a trial, the accused and the accuser both stand. The merchant who cheated and the customer who was cheated - both standing together. The employer who stole time and the employee who was exploited - both present. Every party to every transaction will be there.

And they stand **لِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ** - before the Lord of all the worlds, Master of all nations and peoples. This is the ultimate equalizer. No matter how powerful the corporation, how influential the merchant, how wealthy the businessman, everyone stands equal before the Master of all creation. The CEO and the minimum wage worker, the landlord and the tenant, the creditor and the debtor - all standing, all accountable, all equal before the only Judge whose verdict matters.

The imagery of trial is now complete: the accused has been summoned (**مَّبْعُوثُونَ**), the date has been set (**لِيَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ**), and now all parties stand in the courtroom before the Judge (**يَوْمَ يَقُومُ ٱلنَّاسُ**). What comes next? The verdict and the sentence.

translation: No! Indeed, the record of the wicked is in sijjeen
commentary:
**كَلَّآ إِنَّ كِتَـٰبَ ٱلْفُجَّارِ لَفِى سِجِّينٍ**

*No! Indeed, the record of the wicked is in sijjin.*

**كَلَّآ** is an emphatic rejection - "No! Absolutely not!" It rejects the delusion that these crimes don't matter, that there will be no accountability, that they've gotten away with it. On the contrary, not only is there accountability, but the records are already being kept.

Notice the shift in terminology. The surah began with **الْمُطَفِّفِينَ** - those who commit minor infractions, who skim just a little. Now Allah ﷻ calls them **الْفُجَّارِ** - the wicked, those guilty of major transgression. Why the change? Because while they think they've committed a trivial offense, barely worth mentioning, Allah ﷻ reveals its true magnitude. **فَجَّارَ** comes from **فَجَرَ**, meaning to burst forth, to explode, to tear open violently. A **فَاجِر** is someone whose evil explodes outward uncontrollably. That "minor" business fraud? From Allah's perspective, it's a major crime worthy of this severe designation.

The word **كِتَـٰبَ** - record - is crucial. These criminals didn't keep clear records of their dealings; they operated in the shadows, leaving no paper trail. But Allah ﷻ is keeping immaculate records. Everything is documented. The **كِتَـٰبَ ٱلْفُجَّارِ** - the record of the wicked - exists right now, not someday in the future. The verdict has already been written.

And where is this record kept? **فِى سِجِّينٍ**. The word **سِجِّينٍ** is most commonly understood to mean prison - specifically, the prison of Hell, or a particularly terrible section of it. The word evokes confinement, restriction, inescapability. We've moved from the courtroom to the prison. The trial is over, the verdict is in, and now we're shown where the convicted will be held.

Some scholars say sijjin is the name of a specific place in Hell. Others say it's the name of the actual record book itself - a registry kept at the gates of Hell, like the prison log where every inmate is documented upon arrival. Either way, the image is clear: before the gates of Hell, there's a registry, and when these criminals arrive, their names will be found there, their crimes already recorded, their sentences already set.

translation: And what can make you know what is sijjeen
commentary:
**وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا سِجِّينٌ**

*And what can make you know what is sijjin?*

This is a rhetorical question expressing the incomprehensibility of sijjin. **مَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ** - what could possibly give you knowledge of this? It's beyond human comprehension, beyond our ability to fully grasp. But this question also serves another purpose: it's an expression of anger. When someone is furious and asks "Did you hear what I just said?" they don't want an answer. The question itself is the statement.

Similarly, when Allah ﷻ asks **وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا سِجِّينٌ**, He's not necessarily expecting or providing a complete answer. The question emphasizes the severity, the mystery, the terror of what awaits. You think you understand consequences? You have no idea what sijjin really is. You cannot fathom the horror that awaits those whose records are kept there.

translation: It is [their destination recorded in] a register inscribed
commentary:
**كِتَـٰبٌ مَّرْقُومٌ**

*It is a [thoroughly] inscribed record.*

After the rhetorical question, Allah ﷻ returns to describing the record itself. **كِتَـٰبٌ**- it's a book, a record, a register. But not just any record: **مَّرْقُومٌ**.

**رَقْمٌ** means etched, carved, engraved. When something is carved into stone or metal, it cannot be erased. But **مَّرْقُومٌ** adds another dimension. **رُقُوم** refers to embroidery, to writing that rises above the surface, that you can feel with your fingers - like Braille, or like embroidered text on fabric. When something is printed, it can smudge, it can fade. But when something is embroidered, stitched into the very fabric, no amount of washing will remove it.

**كِتَـٰبٌ مَّرْقُومٌ** - this is a record that is carved, embroidered, raised, permanent, inerasable, backed up beyond any possibility of deletion. In modern terms, this is data stored with such redundancy that even if every server in the world crashed, it would remain intact. This record will never be lost, never corrupted, never expunged. The crimes are documented permanently, preserved for judgment.

translation: Woe, that Day, to the deniers
commentary:
**وَيْلٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ**

*Woe, that Day, to the deniers.*

The surah returns to where it began: **وَيْلٌ** - woe, destruction, curse. But now with an addition: **يَوْمَئِذٍ** - on that Day, the Day we've been describing, the Day of Standing before the Lord of all the worlds.

And who receives this woe? **لِّلْمُكَذِّبِينَ**. This is a crucial shift in understanding. **مُكَذِّبِينَ** doesn't mean "liars" - that would be  **مُكَذِّبِينَ** .**كَـٰذِبِينَ** means those who call others liars, who accuse others of lying, who deny the truth of what they're told.

In context, these are the merchants and service providers who, when confronted by the customers they've cheated, call those customers liars. "What are you talking about? You're lying. I gave you exactly what you paid for." They do **تكذيب** of their victims, dismissing and invalidating their legitimate complaints.

But there's a deeper level to this **تكذيب**. When you call someone a liar even though you know they're telling the truth, when you deny what you know to be fact, you can only do so because you're absolutely confident you'll never face consequences. You will only boldly call your cheated customer a liar if you're certain this will never come back to haunt you, that no trial awaits, that no judgment will occur. In doing **تكذيب** of your customer, you are actually doing **تكذيب** of the Day of Judgment itself. You are denying, through your actions, that you will ever be held accountable.

This is the critical insight: there's theoretical disbelief in the Afterlife - someone says "I don't believe in judgment." Then there's practical disbelief - someone whose behavior demonstrates they don't believe in judgment regardless of what they claim with their words. Actions speak louder than words, and the behavior of the mutaffifin screams their disbelief in accountability.

translation: Who deny the Day of Recompense
commentary:
**ٱلَّذِينَ يُكَذِّبُونَ بِيَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ**

*Who deny the Day of Recompense.*

Now it's made explicit: these mukadhibin are specifically those who deny **يَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ** - the Day of Recompense, the Day of Judgment, the Day when all accounts are settled.

The word **دِّينِ** encompasses several meanings: religion, judgment, recompense, accountability, settlement of debt. **يَوْمِ ٱلدِّينِ** is the day when every debt is paid, every wrong is addressed, every injustice is rectified, every account is balanced. To deny this day is to believe you can cheat, steal, oppress and face no consequences.

But crucially, this ayah clarifies that what we've been discussing all along is fundamentally about faith in the Afterlife. The surah hasn't been merely criticizing bad business practices; it's been revealing that those practices stem from and demonstrate a broken relationship with faith in Allah's ultimate justice.

translation: And none deny it except every sinful transgressor
commentary:
**وَمَا يُكَذِّبُ بِهِۦٓ إِلَّا كُلُّ مُعْتَدٍ أَثِيمٍ**

*And none deny it except every sinful transgressor.*

This ayah answers the implicit question: what kind of person could possibly deny the Day of Judgment? Allah ﷻ tells us: **إِلَّا كُلُّ مُعْتَدٍ أَثِيمٍ** - none except every transgressor, steeped in sin.

**مُعْتَدٍ** comes from **عُدْوَان**, meaning aggression and transgression. This is someone who constantly crosses boundaries, who violates the rights of others - whether verbally, financially or physically. They see limits and deliberately exceed them. They see rights and intentionally violate them.

**أَثِيمٍ** is even more profound. **إِثْمٍ** means sin, but also means the penalty you must pay for that sin. An **أَثِيمٍ** isn't someone who commits a sin and then repents; it's someone who is perpetually in a state of sin, someone whose very existence has become saturated with wrongdoing.

How can someone be "always in sin"? What about when they're sleeping? Here's the insight: when you've cheated someone in business, the harm you caused continues even while you sleep. The loss they suffered because of you compounds over time. Every grain of rice they didn't receive continues to be absent from their pantry. Every penny you took that wasn't yours, and every subsequent profit you built upon those stolen pennies, continues to generate sin. You're sleeping in a house purchased with **haram** money, breathing air inside walls built with oppression. The **ithm** never stops accumulating.

And when someone reaches this state - when they're so immersed in transgression and sin that it defines them - then any reminder, any counsel, any attempt to warn them falls on deaf ears.

translation: When Our verses are recited to him, he says, "Legends of the former peoples
commentary:
**إِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَـٰتُنَا قَالَ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ**

*When Our verses are recited to him, he says, "Legends of the former peoples."*

This is the manifestation of their **تكذيب**. **إِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِ ءَايَـٰتُنَا** - when the ayat of Allah ﷻ are recited to him, when he's given clear evidence, when the Quran itself speaks to his situation, warns him, reminds him of nations before him who committed similar crimes and faced destruction - what is his response?

**قَالَ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ** - he dismisses it all as "legends of the ancients," mythology, fairy tales. The word **أَسَـٰطِيرُ** comes from the root that gives us the English word "story." To him, the Quran is just ancient stories - tales of dragons and wizards, irrelevant mythology from long ago.

"A nation got destroyed for their sins? Please. Save your fairy tales for someone else. I'm not interested in your storytelling. What do these ancient legends have to do with me and my business?"

When Muslims today say "the Quran just contains a lot of old stories that don't really apply to modern life," they are literally echoing the words of those who denied revelation in the first place. This dismissal of the Quran as irrelevant historical tales is not analysis or criticism - it's the exact response of those being condemned in this surah.

translation: No! Rather, the stain has covered their hearts of that which they were earning
commentary:
**كَلَّا ۖ بَلْ ۜ رَانَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ**

*No! Rather, the stain has covered their hearts of that which they were earning.*

**كَلَّا** - No! Absolutely not! The Quran's dismissal of the dismissal. When they say "these are just old stories," Allah ﷻ responds: that's not the problem at all. The problem isn't with the message; the problem is with the receiver.

**بَلْ رَانَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِم** - rather, a **ران** has covered their hearts. **ران** refers to rust, to a dark staining film that gradually accumulates and hardens on a surface. It's both a seal that prevents anything from entering and a filth that corrupts what's underneath.

And what caused this rust? **مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَكْسِبُونَ** - that which they were earning. Every penny earned through cheating, every profit made through deception, every margin gained through oppression - each one added another layer of rust to their hearts.

The Prophet ﷺ was asked about this ayah and explained: "A servant commits a sin and a black dot appears on his heart. If he repents, seeks Allah's forgiveness and desists, his heart is polished. But if he returns to sin, the blackness increases until it covers his entire heart. That is the **ران** mentioned in this ayah."

These people have the money to polish and beautify everything in their lives - their homes, their businesses, their possessions. Everything gleams except the one thing that matters most: their hearts grow darker and dirtier by the day. They can remove rust from their gates and polish their marble floors, but the rust on their hearts only deepens.

When the heart reaches this state - sealed, darkened, covered in the accumulated rust of repeated sins - it loses all sensitivity. It can no longer feel shame, guilt or remorse. It cannot humble itself, cannot observe itself honestly, cannot accept advice or criticism. And when the Book of Allah ﷻ comes to awaken its conscience, the rusted heart dismisses it: "Just old stories."

The **ران** isn't the reason they criticize the Quran; it's the explanation for why they're unable to receive its guidance. Their hearts have been sealed by their own earnings, their own choices, their own repeated returns to the same transgressions without repentance.

translation: No! Indeed, from their Lord, that Day, they will be partitioned
commentary:
**كَلَّآ إِنَّهُمْ عَن رَّبِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَّمَحْجُوبُونَ**

*No! Indeed, from their Lord, that Day, they will be partitioned.*

**كَلَّآ** appears again - another emphatic rejection. They think they've gotten away with it, that the rust on their hearts doesn't matter, that their dismissal of revelation is justified. No! The consequences are real and devastating.

**إِنَّهُمْ عَن رَّبِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَّمَحْجُوبُونَ** - they will be veiled from their Lord on that Day, barricaded, partitioned, separated. The word **مَحْجُوبُونَ** suggests a barrier, a veil, a partition that prevents access.

The imagery is devastating: in this life, they created barriers around their hearts through their earnings. They veiled themselves from divine guidance, from the Quran's message, from their own conscience. On Judgment Day, they will discover that Allah ﷻ has responded in kind. Just as they pushed Him away in this world, He will push them away in the next.

The punishment mirrors the crime. They barricaded their hearts; their hearts will be barricaded from mercy. They created distance from Allah ﷻ through their behavior; they will experience infinite distance from Him when they desperately need His gaze most.

And this ayah has been understood by scholars as evidence that believers will indeed see Allah ﷻ on the Day of Judgment. If the wicked are specifically barricaded from seeing their Lord, the logical implication is that the righteous will not be barricaded - they will be granted that vision. As Allah ﷻ says elsewhere: **وُجُوهٌ يَوْمَئِذٍ نَّاضِرَةٌ ۝ إِلَىٰ رَبِّهَا نَاظِرَةٌ** - "Faces that Day will be radiant, looking at their Lord." (75:22-23)

May Allah ﷻ grant us that beautiful vision and protect us from the horror of being veiled from Him.

translation: Then indeed, they will [enter and] burn in Hellfire
commentary:
**ثُمَّ إِنَّهُمْ لَصَالُوا۟ ٱلْجَحِيمِ**

*Then indeed, they will [enter and] burn in Hellfire.*

After the spiritual punishment of being barricaded from Allah ﷻ comes the physical punishment. **ثُمَّ** - then, afterward - indicates sequence. First the devastating realization that they are cut off from their Lord, then the physical torment.

**إِنَّهُمْ لَصَالُوا۟ ٱلْجَحِيمِ** - they will certainly burn in the Jahim. The word **صَالُوا۟** is often translated simply as "they will enter" but its root meaning is more specific. **صَلَى** originally meant to become soft, particularly when fire makes meat tender. **لَصَالُوا۟** suggests they will be cast into the fire and begin to soften, to melt, to be broken down by its intensity.

This isn't merely entering a location; it's the beginning of disintegration in the roaring flame. **ٱلْجَحِيمِ** is one of the names of Hell, emphasizing its intensity, its roaring, its blazing fury.

The **لام** of emphasis - **لَصَالُوا۟** - makes the statement certain, undeniable, inevitable. There is no doubt, no possibility of escape. They will absolutely, certainly face this torment.

May Allah ﷻ protect us and all Muslims from even approaching this fate.

translation: Then it will be said [to them], "This is what you used to deny
commentary:
**ثُمَّ يُقَالُ هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى كُنتُم بِهِۦ تُكَذِّبُونَ**

*Then it will be said [to them], "This is what you used to deny."*

The final humiliation, the ultimate proof. **ثُمَّ يُقَالُ** - then it will be said to them. After they're veiled from Allah ﷻ, after they're cast into the roaring flame, they will hear these words:

**هَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى كُنتُم بِهِۦ تُكَذِّبُونَ** - "This is what you used to deny." This is that Day of Judgment you called a myth. This is that accountability you dismissed as fairy tales. This is that consequence you never thought would come. This is real. All of it.

Remember how many times **تكذيب** appeared in this passage? They did **تكذيب** of their customers who complained. They did **تكذيب** of the Day of Recompense. They did **تكذيب** of the Quranic verses reminding them. Now they will hear: this thing you kept calling a lie - it's real. You're experiencing it. Is it still legend? Is it still mythology? Does it feel real now?

Allah ﷻ even asks this question explicitly elsewhere in the Quran: **أَفَبِهَـٰذَا ٱلْحَدِيثِ أَنتُم مُّدْهِنُونَ** - "Is this statement unreal to you?" (56:81) When they taste it, they'll know. But that knowledge will come too late.

---

This first section of Surah Al-Mutaffifin establishes a foundation that many miss: the Quran's rage here isn't initially about disbelievers versus believers, Muslims versus non-Muslims. The fundamental message is about justice - Allah's ﷻ standard of justice that applies to all of humanity.

When human beings engage in injustice against other human beings, it enrages Allah ﷻ regardless of the faith of the perpetrator or victim. The surah begins with business ethics, with fair dealing, with giving people their rights - matters that transcend religious boundaries. Only later does it connect these ethical failures to deeper theological problems: denial of the Day of Judgment, rejection of divine guidance, rust accumulating on the heart.

The lesson is clear: you cannot claim faith in Allah ﷻ, in His revelation, in the Day of Judgment while simultaneously cheating His creation. Your behavior toward people is evidence of your belief in accountability. If you deny people their rights, you are denying the Day when all rights will be restored. If you oppress others, you are demonstrating that you don't really believe you'll face the Oppressed Lord on a day when oppression will be rectified.

Allah ﷻ created everything with balance and measure - **ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ ۝ عَلَّمَ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ ۝ خَلَقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنَ ۝ عَلَّمَهُ ٱلْبَيَانَ ۝ ٱلشَّمْسُ وَٱلْقَمَرُ بِحُسْبَانٍ** - He raised the heavens and established the balance. The entire creation operates on scales of justice, on precise measures. We are taught not to cheat those scales, not to violate that balance - **وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلْوَزْنَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ وَلَا تُخْسِرُوا۟ ٱلْمِيزَانَ** - "Establish weight in justice and do not make deficient the balance." (55:9)

May Allah ﷻ make us people of justice in all our dealings, protect us from oppressing others or being oppressed, purify our hearts from the rust of sin, and grant us the vision of His Noble Face on the Day of Judgment.

translation: No! Indeed, the record of the righteous is in 'illiyyun
commentary:
**كَلَّآ إِنَّ كِتَـٰبَ ٱلْأَبْرَارِ لَفِى عِلِّيِّينَ**

*No! Indeed, the record of the righteous is in 'Illiyyin.*

The surah now makes a powerful transition. After describing the record of the sinful—those called fujjar instead of simply being labeled sijjin—Allah ﷻ shifts our attention to the record of the righteous. The book of good people, الأبرار, is not in some dungeon or prison. Instead, it resides in عِلِّيِّينَ.

The word 'illiyyin presents an interesting linguistic challenge. It derives from علو, which means height or elevation. What makes this term particularly significant is its grammatical form—it uses the sound plural ending (ين and ون), which is typically reserved for beings possessing intellect: people, angels or jinn. This grammatical construction points us toward one of the most natural interpretations: the record of good people exists in the company of the highest angels.

To understand this fully, we need to trace the continuity across the surahs in this part of the Quran. Earlier, we learned about the highest angels whose job is to write the revelation—described as سَفَرَةٍ كِرَامٍ بَرَرَةٍ (noble and dutiful scribes). That record is then given to Jibreel (AS), who delivers it to the Messenger ﷺ. The Quran itself describes this: إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلُ رَسُولٍ كَرِيمٍ (Indeed, the Quran is the word of a noble messenger), referring to the angel who brings the book written by other angels and delivers it to the Prophet ﷺ.

Now, in this passage, we're taken back to the heavens and shown that there exists another group of exalted angels who possess another sacred book—the book of our deeds, the record of righteous actions. Throughout the Makkan Quran especially, keep your attention on two books: the Book of Revelation and the Book of Deeds. Allah ﷻ often compares and contrasts these two records.

Some scholars understood this to mean that when a righteous person dies, their soul is taken up to be among the 'illiyyin. Imagine a vast record containing all the good people and their deeds. The angels open to your page and show you: "This is you. You did good. You're going to be all right. Just relax now, but you have to go back into your grave for now. We just wanted to let you know you're doing okay." You're given this assurance and then returned, carrying this guarantee with you.

Others interpreted 'illiyyin as referring to the highest levels of Jannah itself. Not only do you go to Paradise—your record goes there too. This connects to other passages where, on the Day of Judgment, believers walk around displaying their records with joy, saying "Read my record! Look at what I did! Look at my score!"

But let's bring this back to the immediate audience hearing these words. The believers in Makkah were considered nobody. They were the weak, the marginalized, those with no immigration status, no financial standing, no political power. They were the ones who could be spat upon, talked down to, dismissed at will. They were the customers who got cheated. Yet despite their low worldly status, they were good people.

Now Allah ﷻ tells them that all the good they have done has been recorded. Others may dismiss them, but Allah ﷻ does not. Here's what's remarkable: the people considered the lowest in society—the أراذل (scum), the insignificant group—are elevated by Allah ﷻ to be among the highest. The muttaffifin, the cheaters, are the millionaires, the business owners, those with money and status. But the believers who have nothing in worldly terms? Their record is so elevated that it exists in the company of the angels closest to Allah ﷻ.

translation: And what can make you know what is 'illiyyun
commentary:
**وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا عِلِّيُّونَ**

*And what can make you know what is 'Illiyyin?*

Allah ﷻ poses this rhetorical question to emphasize the magnificence of 'illiyyin. What could possibly give you any clue about what these highest realms are? The question itself suggests that this reality is beyond our current comprehension—we can grasp at its meaning, but its full reality exceeds our earthly understanding.

translation: It is [their destination recorded in] a register inscribed
commentary:
**كِتَـٰبٌ مَّرْقُومٌ**

*It is a register inscribed.*

The word مَرْقُومٌ describes something that is perfectly stitched, permanently bound, inscribed with precision. This record isn't going anywhere. It's secure, permanent, and perfectly maintained. Every good deed has been documented with care and will never be lost or forgotten.

translation: Which is witnessed by those brought near [to Allah]
commentary:
**يَشْهَدُهُ ٱلْمُقَرَّبُونَ**

*Which is witnessed by those brought near [to Allah].*

المُقَرَّبُونَ are the angels closest to Allah ﷻ. They witness and testify to this record. When you stand on the Day of Judgment, these angels will actually serve as witnesses in your favor. They'll testify: "Yes, we witnessed it. His record was there. It was among us."

Think about what this means for believers struggling in the present moment. You may be here on earth, considered insignificant, but the record of your deeds is so honored that it exists in a place worthy of being close to Allah ﷻ. Your good actions have such a high place with Him that they're kept in the company of His most honored angels.

translation: Indeed, the righteous will be in pleasure
commentary:
**إِنَّ ٱلْأَبْرَارَ لَفِى نَعِيمٍ**

*Indeed, the righteous will be in pleasure.*

Now Allah ﷻ takes us into Paradise itself. Notice He uses the present-tense form—this is called الحال in Arabic, employed to make you feel like something is already happening, as if it's so certain that it's essentially present reality.

The phrase لَفِى نَعِيمٍ means they are surrounded by, immersed in, even drowning in blessings. Wherever they look, there's something new to be amazed by. You're sitting with a friend in Jannah, and your friend says, "You see that?" You look and think, "Whoa, that's amazing!" Then they point elsewhere: "Look at this!" Everywhere your gaze falls, there's some new blessing you hadn't noticed before. You're surrounded by them from all sides.

Notice also that Allah ﷻ consistently uses الأبرار for good people. This is what's called a جمع قلة —a plural form indicating a smaller number. When the Quran wants to indicate many, it uses forms like أبرار or بررة, which it does use for angels. But for people, it consistently uses abrar, suggesting not many.

This is actually a word of hope. It doesn't mean there are only a few good people in the world. Rather, it means that even when you're surrounded by many who are doing wrong, and you're among only a few holding onto what's right, you're still considered among the abrar.

What makes them good? Interestingly, this surah hasn't yet explicitly mentioned that they pray, fast, or perform other acts of worship. What makes them righteous here is simply that they don't do what the first category did—they don't cheat, they don't short-change people, they don't disregard consequences. When you don't engage in those practices, you become among the abrar, and you're surrounded in blessings.

Consider this: when you don't cheat, you're actually missing out on potential profits, aren't you? The cheaters make more money than you because you chose not to deceive. Yet Allah ﷻ says those who didn't cheat, who held their principles—they won't experience any shortage. They'll be surrounded by blessings. They didn't miss out on anything.

Don't even put false information on your resume. Don't inflate two years of experience into three because it sounds better. Don't provide fake references or ask a friend to pretend to be your former manager. Avoid even these seemingly small forms of deception. When you hold onto these principles, the luxuries of Paradise await you.

translation: On adorned couches, observing
commentary:
**عَلَى ٱلْأَرَآئِكِ يَنظُرُونَ**

*On adorned couches, observing.*

The word أَرَائِك (singular أريكة) refers to something more specific than ordinary seating. It's not just any couch or chair. An arikah is something beautified, decorated, well-cushioned—something you can recline on in complete comfort. Think of an extremely expensive, luxurious couch, not something basic. These believers are leaning back on these couches, completely at ease.

يَنظُرُونَ means they're staring, looking intently for an extended time. You don't use this verb for a quick glance. When you see something truly beautiful, something you can't take your eyes off of—a stunning car that pulls up, an exquisite watch on display, a breathtaking piece of art—you don't just glance and move on. You stare.

Here's something beautiful to consider: if you're reclined back, your view is naturally limited. If you really want to look at something, you normally have to sit up uncomfortably and crane your neck. But Allah ﷻ has situated these couches in such a way that you're completely reclined, at perfect ease, and yet you can still get a full view of all the blessings around you. That's the nature of Jannah. Maybe the couches rotate—we'll find out one day, insha'Allah.

translation: You will recognize in their faces the radiance of pleasure
commentary:
**تَعْرِفُ فِى وُجُوهِهِمْ نَضْرَةَ ٱلنَّعِيمِ**

*You will recognize in their faces the radiance of pleasure.*

As they're leaning back taking in this incredible view, you can see on their faces a freshness, a glow. نَضْرَة in Arabic describes fresh food—produce that looks vibrant and alive. It also describes someone who looks well-rested, genuinely happy, radiantly content. When you see someone and think, "Wow, you look different today—you look amazing," that's nadrah.

The faces of these believers are lit up—not just generally, but نَضْرَةَ النَّعِيمِ —lit up by the blessings themselves. They see one gift from Allah ﷻ and their face lights up. They turn and see another blessing, and their face lights up all over again. They look somewhere else, and again their face glows with fresh amazement. It's like watching children on Eid when they receive gifts—pure, uncontainable joy. But here, every direction your eye moves brings a new nadrah to your face, a new glow you can't even hide.

And remarkably, this is all happening while you're just sitting. You haven't touched anything yet. You haven't experienced any gift directly. You're simply chilled back on your couch, and just the sight of what's around you produces this overwhelming joy.

translation: They will be given to drink [pure] wine [which was] sealed
commentary:
**يُسْقَوْنَ مِن رَّحِيقٍ مَّخْتُومٍ**

*They will be given to drink [pure] wine [which was] sealed.*

As you're relaxing in this state of amazement, VIP service begins. يُسْقَوْنَ uses the passive form—"you will be served drink." You're so relaxed and comfortable, leaning back, that you don't need to get up for anything. No, the drink will be brought to you. Service will come to you.

What will they serve? رَحِيق —in Arabic, this means the most expensive wine, the finest quality, the purest available. But here's what's fascinating: this is رَحِيقٍ مَّخْتُومٍ —sealed wine. The word مَخْتُوم comes from ختم, meaning to seal, to close completely. When something is filled to capacity, it's sealed. This is why the Prophet ﷺ is called خَاتَمُ النَّبِيِّينَ —the Seal of the Prophets. By designating him ﷺ as the seal, Allah ﷻ meant this world has been filled with prophets; there's no more room; the line of prophethood is sealed.

Similarly here, these bottles have been filled to the brim with the finest drink and then sealed, corked. If you know anything about fine wine, you know the older it is, the more expensive it becomes. These drinks have been waiting, already aged, already perfected, sealed and ready for the believers.

translation: The last of it is musk. So for this let the competitors compete
commentary:
**خِتَـٰمُهُۥ مِسْكٌ ۚ وَفِى ذَٰلِكَ فَلْيَتَنَافَسِ ٱلْمُتَنَـٰفِسُونَ**

*The last of it is musk. So for this let the competitors compete.*

Picture this: you've got this bottle, perfectly aged, waiting for you. You're about to experience something extraordinary. You take the bottle and smell the cork, the seal on top. And Allah ﷻ says خِتَـٰمُهُۥ مِسْكٌ —its seal is musk itself.

Now, the word خِتَام doesn't just mean something sealed—it could also mean something scented. But He doesn't say the seal is "scented with musk" (which would be مَمْسُوك or مُسَكٌّ). He says the seal IS musk. This means when you smell it, you'll feel like you've never smelled anything before in your existence. The word مِسْك encompasses the very notion of scent, the essence of fragrance itself. That experience is what you call a true scent.

And you haven't even tasted the drink yet! Notice the theme developing: in Jannah, you're looking at something you haven't touched, and your face is glowing. Here, you have a bottle you haven't drunk from—you've only smelled the seal—and that alone overwhelms you with joy.

Then comes one of the most beautiful phrases in the Quran: وَفِى ذَٰلِكَ فَلْيَتَنَافَسِ ٱلْمُتَنَـٰفِسُونَ —"And for that, then let those who compete, compete."

The word تَنَافُس comes from نَفِيس , meaning something elegant, exquisite, remarkable, expensive—a grand prize. When you're running toward something truly magnificent, that's tanafus. Competing in the Olympics is a kind of tanafus because there's a grand prize at the end. The word also connects to نَفَس, meaning breath—suggesting that for this purpose, people should compete with each other until they're losing their breath. They should work hard toward it, value it, struggle for it.

Look at what Allah ﷻ has done: He transported us into Jannah. He made us imagine ourselves there, feel the bottle in our hands, smell the cork. Then He brings us back to reality and asks: Isn't that worth fighting for? Isn't that worth your struggle? And then, after making His point, He takes us right back into Paradise again.

translation: And its mixture is of Tasneem
commentary:
**وَمِزَاجُهُۥ مِن تَسْنِيمٍ**

*And its mixture is of Tasnim.*

Now you've opened the bottle. You've had a sip. مِزَاج in Arabic means mixture, blend. It's what you get when you take two different drinks and mix them together to create something new—a cocktail, literally. So you taste this drink, and there's one flavor you recognize, but then there's something else—something you've never experienced before. A flavor unlike anything you've ever tasted.

Since you're wondering what that mysterious additional flavor is, Allah ﷻ tells you: وَمِزَاجُهُۥ مِن تَسْنِيمٍ—that mix you're tasting, that element that's blowing your mind, comes from something called تَسْنِيم (Tasnim).

What is Tasnim? The word comes from سَنَام, which means the hump of a camel—the highest point. It indicates a very high place. Remember, the record of good people is where? In a very high place—فِى عِلِّيِّينَ (fi 'illiyyin), among the highest angels, witnessed by المُقَرَّبُونَ, those brought closest to Allah ﷻ.

Now these righteous people have this drink. They've popped the cork, sipped it, and tasted something extraordinary. Naturally, they ask: "Where did this come from? Where is this drink being refined? Which spring provides this? This is just a few drops mixed in my drink, and it made it incredible. I want to experience that source directly. Where is Tasnim?"

The name itself—because of the word sanam suggesting height—indicates this is one of the highest places in Jannah. There's a spring there, positioned in an exalted location, and that's why it's called Tasnim.

translation: A spring from which those near [to Allah] drink
commentary:
**عَيْنًا يَشْرَبُ بِهَا ٱلْمُقَرَّبُونَ**

*A spring from which those near [to Allah] drink.*

عَيْنًا means a spring, a fountain, a source of flowing water. يَشْرَبُ بِهَا ٱلْمُقَرَّبُونَ —those who have been brought very close to Allah ﷻ get to sit right by this spring and drink directly from Tasnim itself.

You, among the good people, get drops of it mixed in your drink. But those brought nearest to Allah ﷻ sit right by the source and drink from it directly. Those are المُقَرَّبُونَ —the people closest to Him.

In other words, there are two categories of people in Jannah: good people الأَبْرَار and those who have been brought closest المُقَرَّبُونَ. Similarly, elsewhere in the Quran you find أَصْحَابُ الْيَمِينِ, the People of the Right Hand, and السَّابِقُونَ, the Foremost. Here you find the righteous, and then you find those brought near, may Allah ﷻ make us among them.

By this point in the passage, you're thinking: "I was pretty happy with the bottle. I was amazed by the scent. But this Tasnim thing has driven me crazy. I don't just want to be from the abrar—I want to be from the muqarrabun! I want to reach the highest levels of Paradise!"

And with that, the conversation about Jannah comes to a close. This concludes the second major section of the surah.

translation: Indeed, those who committed crimes used to laugh at those who believed
commentary:
**إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا۟ كَانُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ يَضْحَكُونَ**

*Indeed, those who committed crimes used to laugh at those who believed.*

Now begins a very small but powerful section. إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ أَجْرَمُوا۟—"Those who have committed crimes." Wait—have crimes been mentioned so far? Yes, the surah started with criminal behavior: cheating, short-changing, disregarding consequences. But now we're going to see a different kind of criminal behavior.

That first criminal behavior was against customers, against people in transactions. This is a new form of crime: كَانُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ يَضْحَكُونَ —they used to make fun of the believers. Now we're finally addressing disbelievers mocking believers. Notice that this wasn't the subject before. Allah ﷻ made it a bigger priority to talk about justice that applies to everybody first. Only in the second part did He address believers being ridiculed.

Notice also that this ayah isn't even in the present tense—it's in the past tense. كَانُوا, "they used to." Where are we right now in the narrative? You're still in Jannah. You're among those closest to Allah ﷻ, drinking from that amazing spring. You've never been higher. No human being could possibly reach a higher place (other than the Messenger of Allah ﷺ during his ascension). You are at the pinnacle of existence.

And in this moment, you remember when you used to be in such a low place that even disbelievers used to make fun of you. "Remember those days when we were humiliated? When they laughed at us? When they thought of us as garbage? When they used to mock us?" 

The believers' state hasn't changed yet—Bilal (RA) is still in the state he's in, Ammar and his family (RA) are still being tortured. But when they hear these ayahs, they're transported into the future. They're thinking, "One day we're going to look back at this time and remember: they used to laugh at us." That's what Allah ﷻ has accomplished in this ayah.

translation: And when they passed by them, they would exchange derisive glances
commentary:
**وَإِذَا مَرُّوا۟ بِهِمْ يَتَغَامَزُونَ**

*And when they passed by them, they would exchange derisive glances.*

يَتَغَامَزُونَ comes from غَمْز, which means two things in Arabic: to wink at somebody, or to nudge somebody. When believers would walk by, the disbelievers would wink at each other or nudge one another to harass them. "Look—here come the righteous police." That kind of sarcasm.

People don't have to insult you with words. The way they look at you can be deeply humiliating. Some people are very skilled at this—they just look at you with eyes of disgust, say absolutely nothing, and you know they're revolted by you. Human dignity isn't only about words. Words are harmful enough, but the Quran repeatedly describes frowning, staring, scowling—عَبَسَ وَتَوَلَّىٰ (he frowned and turned away), لَا تُصَعِّرْ خَدَّكَ لِلنَّاسِ (do not turn your cheek away from people in contempt). Even the way your cheek swells up when you're angry tells Allah ﷻ that you're full of yourself, that you're being condescending.

So the disbelievers make fun of believers with gestures, glances, and body language.

translation: And when they returned to their people, they would return jesting
commentary:
**وَإِذَا ٱنقَلَبُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِمُ ٱنقَلَبُوا۟ فَكِهِينَ**

*And when they returned to their people, they would return jesting.*

This is a striking ayah. وَإِذَا ٱنقَلَبُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَهْلِهِمُ —when they go back to their families, these disbelievers who just finished mocking believers, talking down to them, making gestures to belittle them—ٱنقَلَبُوا۟ فَكِهِينَ —they go back very happy, jesting, entertained.

فَكِهِينَ comes from فَاكِهَة, like a tasty fruit. It's as if they can taste the insult they gave, as if it's food to them. Some people genuinely enjoy insulting others—they're hungry for it, they love it, they live for making someone feel small. Scholars say they truly savor the mockery they dish out, deriving pleasure from it like one enjoys delicious food.

But the worst part is this: they go back—they return to their spouses, they return to their children—and they proudly tell them about these incidents. They share how they humiliated the believers. They want to create generations of people who hate Islam from the very beginning. This is about passing down bigotry, teaching hatred to the next generation.

translation: And when they saw them, they would say, "Indeed, those are truly lost
commentary:
**وَإِذَا رَأَوْهُمْ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ لَضَآلُّونَ**

*And when they saw them, they would say, "Indeed, those are truly lost."*

When they see believers anywhere—not just when passing by, but whenever they encounter them—they declare publicly, so everyone else can hear: إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ لَضَآلُّونَ —"These people are lost. These people are so misguided."

They turn to the believers and say things like: "Look at them—they don't even know how to do business properly. They're telling us we're doing something wrong? What do they have to show for it? You want to be successful like them? Look where they live. Look at the clothes they're wearing. This is what you aspire to be? These are the lost causes of society. That's what they are."

translation: But they had not been sent as guardians over them
commentary:
**وَمَآ أُرْسِلُوا۟ عَلَيْهِمْ حَـٰفِظِينَ**

*But they had not been sent as guardians over them.*

There are two ways to understand this ayah. The first interpretation is that this is Allah ﷻ speaking: these disbelievers were not sent as guardians or watchmen over the believers. They don't have to make sure the believers are guided. Who appointed them as judges? Who gave them authority to police the believers' choices?

The second interpretation is that this represents the disbelievers' own complaint. They're saying: "Who do these Muslims think they are, commenting on our behavior? They weren't sent to guard over us. They weren't appointed to make sure we do the right thing. Why do they even talk about this stuff? Why do they critique our business practices?"

But the Quran has already answered that question earlier in the surah. Muslims are not حَافِظِين —guardians—over anyone. You already have guardians watching you: وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكُمْ لَحَافِظِينَ كِرَامًا كَاتِبِينَ (And indeed, over you are guardians, noble and recording). You've got angels documenting everything you do. Believers don't need to be guardians over you—Allah ﷻ has already assigned that task to His angels.

translation: So Today those who believed are laughing at the disbelievers
commentary:
**فَٱلْيَوْمَ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْكُفَّارِ يَضْحَكُونَ**

*So Today those who believed are laughing at the disbelievers.*

Now watch what happens when the equation flips. When the disbelievers were making fun of believers, the verb used was كَانُوا —past tense, describing what they "used to do." But when believers respond, it's فَٱلْيَوْمَ ("So Today," meaning on the Day of Judgment.

ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْكُفَّارِ يَضْحَكُونَ —those who believe are laughing at, or observing the state of, the disbelievers.

translation: On adorned couches, observing
commentary:
**عَلَى ٱلْأَرَآئِكِ يَنظُرُونَ**

*On adorned couches, observing.*

This is the second time couches and reclining are mentioned. Why? The first time, believers were enjoying blessings while reclining. Now, in these elevated seats, they're looking down—down at the disbelievers who are registering into their punishments. And they're observing.

But notice the difference. When disbelievers were laughing at believers, Allah ﷻ didn't just say they laughed. He said they winked, they nudged each other, they went back to their families happily boasting, they came back again to declare how lost the believers were. They engaged in elaborate mockery with words, gestures, and actions.

When believers are mentioned laughing on Judgment Day, the only thing described is that they're sitting on couches, staring. يَنظُرُونَ —they're just looking. Nothing more. They feel no need to say anything. They don't need to wink or nudge or mock. They're satisfied because Allah ﷻ has given them so much blessing that they have no desire to make these people feel any worse. The disbelievers have already gotten everything they deserve. The believers don't need to add words now.

Sometimes, when you can't take revenge, all you have are words. But when believers see the state of the disbelievers in the Hereafter, they don't even feel the need for words. Justice has been served. It's already taken care of.

translation: Have the disbelievers [not] been rewarded [this Day] for what they used to do
commentary:
**هَلْ ثُوِّبَ ٱلْكُفَّارُ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ**

*Have the disbelievers [not] been rewarded [this Day] for what they used to do?*

When that scene unfolds, Allah ﷻ asks this rhetorical question: هَلْ ثُوِّبَ ٱلْكُفَّارُ—have disbelievers been paid in full? Have they been fully compensated?

The word ثَوَاب is used for appropriate compensation, suitable payment. And مَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ —the things they used to do. The phrase يَفْعَلُونَ suggests mindless behavior, actions done without thought. If they had thought about their actions and then done them, the verb would be يَعْمَلُونَ, which implies deliberate work. But يَفْعَلُونَ indicates they acted without considering consequences, engaging in behavior they never even reflected upon. Now they're paying for it, finally realizing what they had done.

This question actually connects us back to where the surah started: إِذَا ٱكْتَالُوا۟ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ يَسْتَوْفُونَ (when they take a measure from people, they take in full). The surah began with people demanding full measure for themselves. Now it ends with Allah ﷻ ensuring full measure in recompense: Have they not been fully repaid?

This closing question serves two purposes. First, it addresses the believers in Makkah as they're being tortured and persecuted. Allah ﷻ tells them: Look, it's a done deal. Isn't that enough for you? Don't worry about it. It's coming. It's guaranteed. They won't get away with anything. هَلْ ثُوِّبَ—you think they got away with their crimes? You think they committed these acts and won't pay? They will pay, and it's mentioned in past tense as if it's already happened, as if to say: It's as good as done.

Second, on Judgment Day itself, when you're sitting on that couch looking at the disbelievers receiving their recompense, Allah ﷻ will say: Didn't I take care of you? Didn't I pay them exactly what I promised I would? Everything they did has been fully compensated. Don't you see it? Everything they dismissed has been accounted for. Everything they thought they got away with has been addressed.

That rhetorical question closes the surah—a question that provides both reassurance in this world and vindication in the next.
